title: SBOM vendor-component CVE exposure
exclusions: Does NOT evaluate first-party dependency CVEs (covered by 22.43.1); targets third-party SBOM inputs.
evidence: Saved search 'sbom_cve_exposure' (daily) joins vendor SBOMs against the current CVE feed.

compliance.assurance_rationale: NIS2 supply-chain risk-management clause is supported by SBOM-CVE correlation.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(d) (Supply-chain security) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.43.5: SBOM vendor-component CVE exposure.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.43.5' running on Vendor SBOMs (CycloneDX/SPDX), CVE feed (NVD, vendor advisories)., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: EU Cyber Resilience Act requires product vulnerability management backed by SBOM.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that EU-CRA Art.13 (Obligations of manufacturers) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.43.5: SBOM vendor-component CVE exposure.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.43.5' running on Vendor SBOMs (CycloneDX/SPDX), CVE feed (NVD, vendor advisories)., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

compliance.assurance_rationale: SR-11 component authenticity includes supply-chain vulnerability monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST-800-53 SR-11 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.43.5: SBOM vendor-component CVE exposure.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.43.5' running on Vendor SBOMs (CycloneDX/SPDX), CVE feed (NVD, vendor advisories)., archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Vendor component log4j 2.14 is in the SBOM; CVE-2021-44228 is published; the UC fires on the next run.
controlTest.negativeScenario: No vendor component matches the current CVE feed; no alert.

dataSources: Vendor SBOMs (CycloneDX/SPDX), CVE feed (NVD, vendor advisories). — supplemented by authentication logs, system audit trails, and configuration management database (CMDB) asset inventory exports
spl:
| inputlookup vendor_sbom.csv
| lookup cve_feed.csv component version OUTPUT cve cvss_v3_base
| where isnotnull(cve) AND cvss_v3_base>=7.0
| stats values(cve) as cves, max(cvss_v3_base) as max_cvss by vendor component version
| table vendor component version cves max_cvss
description: Uses the vendor SBOM register to answer 'which vendor components in my estate are affected by the CVE published today?'.
value: Closes the gap between the day a CVE is published and the day a vendor responds — crucial for CRA and NIS2 supply-chain narratives.
implementation: (1) Ingest vendor SBOMs from the procurement workflow; (2) subscribe to NVD feed into cve_feed.csv; (3) run daily; (4) SOAR opens a case per vendor-affected component.
visualization: Table of exposed components, bar chart of CVE count by vendor, single-value 'SBOM-indexed components with no open CVE'.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK — T1195.002 Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Supply Chain

knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Lookups**: `vendor_sbom.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('vendor_sbom.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `Art.21(2)(d)`. The control objective is: "Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(d) (Supply-chain security) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.43.5: SBOM vendor-component CVE exposure." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into the relevant indexes under the relevant sourcetypes, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Ingest vendor SBOMs from the procurement workflow. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 2:** subscribe to NVD feed into cve_feed.csv. Ingestion SLA drift is usually invisible until it breaks something — monitor the feed's freshness with a companion search that alerts when the newest event is older than the expected cadence.

• **Hint 3:** run daily. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** SOAR opens a case per vendor-affected component. Automated remediation must be gated behind a defined approval workflow; compliance controls that take destructive action without human review are themselves a governance risk.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "SBOM vendor-component CVE exposure," the aggregation produces the risk posture — the per-row values should reconcile to the organisation's risk register entries, not stand alone as engineering metrics.

This search begins with `| inputlookup vendor_sbom.csv` — the base dataset is a CSV/KV Store lookup, not live event data. This inverts the usual Splunk assumption: the detection does not stream from an index, it iterates a register. The register itself is the authoritative source of truth (contracts, policies, sign-offs, attestations) and the search joins it against indexed events to find gaps.

Lookup authority: treat `vendor_sbom.csv` as a primary compliance artifact. Its contents ARE the answer the regulator will ask for ('show me your vendor list,' 'show me your CDE inventory,' 'show me your policy exceptions'). Implement the following controls on the lookup itself, not just the search:

• Version the CSV in a git repository with a mandatory pull-request review for every change.
• Maintain a signed-record trail: who changed what, when, and with what approval. A lookup with a silent edit history is an audit liability.
• Automate a nightly diff: `| inputlookup vendor_sbom.csv | eval hash=md5(...) | outputlookup append=true vendor_sbom_audit.csv` — this produces an immutable record of the register's state over time.
• Lock the lookup with role-based access: only the content owner has `list_inputs` + `write_file` on the path; everyone else is read-only.

The live-data side of the join (across the full search range) is the detection trigger — when the register and the telemetry disagree, that's a finding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.43.5: SBOM vendor-component CVE exposure]
cron_schedule = 0 */6 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h@h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.43.5,reg=NIS2
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `vendor`, `component`, `version`, `cve`. Run:

```spl
index=the_index sourcetype=the_sourcetype earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table vendor, component, version, cve
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `cvss_v3_base`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(cvss_v3_base) as p50 perc90(cvss_v3_base) as p90 perc99(cvss_v3_base) as p99 max(cvss_v3_base) as hi
```

The current threshold `7.0` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.43.5: NIS2 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Table Of Exposed Components (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 2 — Bar Chart Of Cve Count By Vendor (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Single-Value 'Sbom-Indexed Components With No Open Cve' (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**NIS2-specific failure modes:**

• 24/7 reporting obligation (Art. 23): NIS2 requires early warning within 24 hours of awareness of a significant incident. Detections that back this SLA must compute elapsed time from the SOC's first-acknowledged timestamp (not the resolve-time) and must trigger independently of business hours — the regulatory clock does not pause overnight.

• Member-state variation: NIS2 is transposed individually by each EU Member State with some latitude on thresholds and sector scoping. A detection correct for German IT-SiG 3 (BSI) may be misaligned with Dutch CWV or French LPM. Use `nis2_member_state_config.csv` to parametrize thresholds per jurisdiction; never hardcode.

• Essential vs Important entity criteria: the applicable obligations depend on sector + size. Before alerting, verify the monitored entity's designation; a detection firing on an out-of-scope subsidiary causes unnecessary regulatory notifications that erode trust with the national CSIRT.

• Supply-chain (Art. 21(2)(d)) extent: NIS2 explicitly includes direct suppliers' cybersecurity posture. Detections must cover monitored-by-you-not-owned-by-you infrastructure (outsourced SaaS, managed SIEM tenants) — these are in scope even if operationally run by a third party. BA/BAA-style governance applies.
grandmaExplanation: We help you see patch timing against your service promises so you can explain delays with evidence.
